ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Shahi Tukda; closest defensible match is bread pudding food product (FOODON_03315091). Shahi Tukda is a South Asian variant involving fried bread, sugar syrup, and rabri (reduced milk).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (bread-based sweet dish) used as closest defensible category. No specific Shahi Tukda entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Shahi Tukda or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Shahi Tukda is approximately 180 g, typically comprising two pieces of fried bread topped with rabri and garnished with slivered nuts.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Shahi Tukda in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for white bread (fried), sugar syrup, and rabri (reduced whole milk), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried bread (USDA FDC ID 172686) and sweetened condensed/reduced milk (USDA FDC ID 171265). Input AI-generated values retained where consistent with component-based estimation. Saturated fat estimated from ghee and whole milk fat contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Shahi Tukda in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 75 estimated by analogy to white bread fried in fat (GI ~70–80, Atkinson et al. 2008) combined with sugar syrup and sweetened reduced milk. The high refined carbohydrate load from white bread and sucrose syrup supports a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 56.16) / 100 ≈ 42.1 g (serving 180 g × 31.2 g carb/100 g = 56.16 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from ghee and rabri contributing to cardiovascular load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Shahi Tukda to occasional small portions (≤90 g) due to its saturated fat content and caloric density, which can contribute to weight gain and elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and very high sugar content (22.5 g/100 g) from sucrose syrup and rabri causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Shahi Tukda is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI, high sugar load, and large glycemic load per serving (~42 g GL); consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to a single small piece on rare occasions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g) from ghee and whole-milk rabri, combined with high caloric density (310 kcal/100 g) and elevated sugar intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or very strictly limit Shahi Tukda, as its high saturated fat and sugar content can raise LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, increasing atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar and saturated fat content are pro-inflammatory dietary factors
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Shahi Tukda as its high refined sugar and saturated fat content may exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shahi Tukda (Hindi: शाही टुकड़ा, meaning 'Royal Morsel') is a classic Mughlai dessert originating from the royal kitchens of the Mughal Empire, particularly associated with the cities of Hyderabad and Lucknow in North India. It is prepared by deep-frying thick slices of white bread in ghee until golden and crisp, then soaking them in a warm saffron- and cardamom-infused sugar syrup. The syrup-soaked bread is topped with rabri — a slow-cooked, thickened reduction of whole milk sweetened with sugar — and finished with a generous garnish of slivered almonds and pistachios, often with a few drops of rose water for fragrance. The result is a layered dessert with contrasting textures: a slightly crisp bread base softened by syrup, blanketed in dense, creamy rabri, and punctuated by the crunch of nuts. Nutritionally, Shahi Tukda is energy-dense at approximately 310 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (~18.5 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from ghee and milk), high sugar (~22.5 g/100 g), and moderate protein (~4.8 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at ~75 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The high saturated fat load is a concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension. It is traditionally served at weddings, Eid celebrations, and festive occasions, and is best consumed as an occasional indulgence in small portions.
common_user_queries: Is Shahi Tukda good for diabetics?
Is Shahi Tukda the same as Double ka Meetha?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Shahi Tukda exists in ICMR-NIN published tables or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component-based analysis using ICMR-NIN data for fried bread, sugar syrup, and rabri, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on fried white bread and sugar syrup. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol are estimated from ghee and whole-milk fat composition data. Water content is not available from the component estimation approach used.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no single approved-source entry for Shahi Tukda exists. 2. Saturated fat (9.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (38 mg/100 g) are estimated from the known fatty acid profiles of ghee (USDA FDC ID 171314) and whole milk (USDA FDC ID 746782) in proportion to their contribution to the dish. 3. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE (82 mcg RAE/100 g), converted from the input value of 110 mcg retinol equivalents using standard RAE conversion. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled Shahi Tukda dish is not available from ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central component data used in this estimation. 5. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on fried white bread and sugar syrup data from Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for Shahi Tukda is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Portions in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (180 g each); deduplicated to a single 180 g serving. 7. The input listed 'low_sodium' as a dietary tag; this has been removed as 120 mg sodium/100 g and a 180 g serving delivering 216 mg sodium does not meet a low-sodium classification. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 (bread pudding food product) and FoodEx2 A0FKP (bread-based sweet dish) used as closest defensible matches; no exact codes exist for Shahi Tukda.
